title.title: New Trends
title.subtitle: ZK rollups, Layer 2 scaling, privacy primitives, and choosing the right platform

questions.title: Who can use it?
questions.subtitle: Anyone in the world, or only known organisations?

options.label: 🌐 Public
options.hint: Anyone with a wallet. No vetting.

options.label: 🏢 Permissioned
options.hint: Only members invited by a consortium.

questions.title: What does it need to do?
questions.subtitle: Just move value, or run logic / apps on-chain?

options.label: ⚡ High-perf consumer apps
options.hint: Order books, games, social — needs speed.

questions.title: How sensitive to throughput + fees?
questions.subtitle: Are you a high-value settlement layer or a consumer app?

options.label: 🐢 Low — settlement only
options.hint: Few txs/day, large value, fees acceptable.

options.label: 🛒 Medium — retail
options.hint: Daily user traffic, fees in cents.

questions.title: Privacy needs?
questions.subtitle: How much of your data must stay off the public ledger?

options.label: 🚪 Selective — known parties
options.hint: Some data shared only between subsets of members.

options.label: 🛡️ Strong — cryptographic
options.hint: Hide amounts, identities, calldata. ZK or shielded.

questions.title: Security & validator model?
questions.subtitle: Inherit a big chain's security, or own your validator set?

options.label: 🛡️ Inherit security
options.hint: L2 / parachain / settle to a big L1.

options.label: 🏛️ Run my own
options.hint: Sovereign chain — full control, must bootstrap validators.

btc.name: Bitcoin
btc.reason: Public, value-only, settlement-grade — Bitcoin is the most credible neutral money network.

ln.name: Bitcoin Lightning
ln.tagline: BTC at retail speed · sub-cent fees
ln.reason: You want public BTC payments but with consumer-grade throughput — Lightning is Bitcoin's L2 for that.

eth.name: Ethereum L1
eth.reason: Public, contract-driven, settlement-grade — Ethereum L1 is the canonical choice for high-value composable DeFi.

arb.name: Ethereum L2 (Arbitrum / Base)
arb.reason: Same EVM, much lower fees, inherits Ethereum security via fraud proofs. The default for medium-throughput dApps.

sol.name: Solana
sol.reason: High throughput + low fees + native ecosystem of consumer-grade dApps. Trade-off: fewer, more powerful validators.

avax.name: Avalanche L1
avax.reason: Sub-second finality with the option to launch a sovereign L1 (subnet) while plugging into the Primary Network.

cosmos.name: Cosmos zone
cosmos.reason: You want full control over consensus + gas + governance, and you're happy to bootstrap your own validators. Cosmos SDK + IBC.

fabric.name: Hyperledger Fabric
fabric.reason: Permissioned membership + channels for selective data sharing — purpose-built for known-party B2B consortia.

stark.name: Starknet (ZK)
stark.reason: Strong cryptographic privacy and programmable Cairo VM with ZK validity proofs. Best fit when privacy + smart contracts both matter.

aztec.name: Aztec Network
aztec.reason: Native encrypted state on Ethereum — every tx hides amount + sender by default. The strongest privacy primitive on EVM-adjacent infra.

xrpl.name: XRP Ledger
xrpl.reason: Payment-optimised L1 with a native DEX and Trust Lines. Great for cross-border settlement, RWA tokenisation, and FX rails.

recommender.heading: Pick the Right Platform — Guided
recommender.intro: Five short questions. Each answer narrows the field. At the end you get the best fit (and one runner-up) for your scenario, with the reasoning shown.
recommender.realityCheckBody: this is a heuristic. Real selection always includes auditor availability, talent pool, regulatory posture, and existing customer footprint. Use this as a starting point, not a final answer.

zkp.heading: Zero-Knowledge Proofs — the one idea
zkp.subtitle: Forget the maths. One sentence and one everyday example are enough to get it.
zkp.oneLinerB: without revealing anything that makes it true.
zkp.exampleSubtitle: Same goal, two ways. Watch what the bouncer learns.
zkp.withoutBodyStrong: name, address, nationality and exact birth date
zkp.withoutCaption: You proved one fact and leaked five.
zkp.next: Next: a thought experiment that makes it click →

zkpCave.heading: Where's Waldo? — ZK made visual
zkpCave.subtitle: The classic intuition. I prove I found Waldo on a huge crowded page — without telling you where he is.

steps.title: The claim
steps.text: I say I found Waldo on this enormous, crowded page. You don't believe me — and I refuse to just point, because that gives my answer away.

steps.title: The proof
steps.text: I cover the whole page with a large opaque sheet that has one tiny Waldo-sized hole, lined up exactly over him. You see Waldo through the hole.

steps.title: You're convinced
steps.text: Waldo is clearly on the page — proven. But the sheet hid the rest, so you still have no idea WHERE on the page he actually is.

zkpCave.punchlineA: you end fully convinced Waldo is there, yet learn
zkpCave.punchlineB: about his location. That gap is "zero-knowledge".
zkpCave.classicBody: "the magic cave": Alice repeatedly exits the side a verifier shouts, proving she knows the password without ever saying it. Repeat enough and luck is ruled out.

zkpProps.heading: ZKP: Three Formal Properties & Blockchain Applications
zkpProps.subtitle: Once you have the intuition, these properties formalise it. The blockchain use cases follow directly from the third property — zero-knowledge.

properties.desc: If the statement is true and Alice is honest, she will always be able to convince Bob. A valid proof always passes.
If the statement is false, Alice cannot cheat and convince Bob — no matter how clever she is. A fake proof always fails.
Bob learns nothing beyond "the statement is true." No extra information leaks — not accidentally, not even in theory.

uses.title: Private identity & KYC
uses.desc: Prove your passport is valid, your sanctions check passed, and your age is ≥ 18 — without showing any document. One ZK credential, verifiable by any dApp anywhere.

uses.title: Private voting
uses.desc: Prove your vote was counted in the final tally, and that you only voted once — without revealing which candidate you chose. Fully auditable election, fully private ballot.

uses.title: Proof of solvency
uses.desc: A bank or exchange proves it holds enough assets to cover all liabilities — without disclosing individual account balances, trading positions, or counterparty identities.

uses.title: ZK rollups (Starknet ↗)
uses.desc: Prove that 10,000 transactions are all valid — with a single compact STARK proof that Ethereum L1 verifies in one block. No waiting, no challenge window, no trust in the sequencer.

zkpProps.snarkLabel: ZK-SNARK — Succinct Non-interactive ARgument of Knowledge
zkpProps.snarkBody: Tiny proofs, extremely fast to verify. Requires a one-time trusted setup ceremony. Used by Zcash, Polygon zkEVM, early zkSync.
zkpProps.starkLabel: ZK-STARK — Scalable Transparent ARgument of Knowledge
zkpProps.starkBodyA: No trusted setup, quantum-resistant. Proofs are larger but fully transparent and more scalable. Used by

starknet.heading: Starknet: ZK-Rollup on Ethereum
starknet.subtitle: Batches thousands of transactions off-chain, generates a cryptographic STARK proof, and posts only that proof to Ethereum L1.
starknet.whyLabel: Why ZK has to be the next step
starknet.whyBodyA: Ethereum L1 can't host applications that need
starknet.whyBodyC: . Optimistic rollups solve cost only; ZK rollups also unlock
starknet.whyBodyD: — a user can prove a fact

cards.title: Throughput
cards.desc: 500–1,000 TPS vs ~15 TPS on Ethereum L1. Transactions execute off-chain in batches — the STARK prover is the bottleneck, not the EVM.

cards.title: Cost
cards.desc: 10–100× cheaper for end users. One STARK proof amortises the L1 settlement cost across thousands of transactions in the same batch.

cards.title: Security model
cards.desc: Inherits Ethereum L1 security via validity proofs. No fraud window, no trust in the sequencer — if the proof is invalid, Ethereum rejects it outright.

cards.title: Cairo language
cards.desc: Starknet's native language, compiled to Sierra IR. Far more efficient than Solidity/EVM for STARK proving — ZK-friendly logic without low-level circuit expertise.

news.label: Onchain Gaming
news.sub: Dojo engine — fully on-chain games in Cairo

starknetCompare.heading: Starknet: ZK in Practice
starknetCompare.subtitle: A concrete KYC walkthrough using Starknet's ZK credentials, then a head-to-head with optimistic rollups — the most common comparison when evaluating L2s.
starknetCompare.kycLabel: 🔐 Concrete example — KYC without sharing your identity

kycSteps.label: Bank issues a credential
kycSteps.desc: Your bank verifies you once and signs a ZK credential containing { name, DOB, residency, sanctions check }. This credential lives in your wallet — only you hold it.

kycSteps.label: You generate a proof
kycSteps.desc: In your wallet, you build a STARK proof: "credential is signed by Bank · age ≥ 18 · residency = EU · sanctions = false." No raw data is included in the proof itself.

kycSteps.label: Cairo contract verifies
kycSteps.desc: The dApp's Cairo contract on Starknet verifies the proof and grants access. Your name and birth date never leave your wallet — not even the dApp operator can read them.

starknetCompare.kycFootStrong: private voting · proof of solvency · medical records · sanctions screening
starknetCompare.kycFootB: — anywhere the question is "is this true?" without "who are you?".

zk.title: ZK Rollup
zk.introB: that Ethereum L1 verifies mathematically. No trust in the operator required.

rows.value: Validity proof — mathematically proven

rows.label: Finality
rows.value: ⚡ Minutes — one L1 block to verify the proof

rows.label: Compute overhead
rows.value: High — STARK/SNARK generation is expensive

rows.label: EVM compatibility
rows.value: ⚠️ Partial — zkEVM spectrum still maturing

rows.label: Trust model
rows.value: Trustless — math is the arbiter, not watchers

rows.label: Best for
rows.value: Payments, privacy apps, high-security finance

op.title: Optimistic Rollup
op.introB: of validity. A 7-day challenge window lets anyone submit a fraud proof.

rows.label: Proof type
rows.value: Fraud proof — only if fraud detected

rows.label: Finality
rows.value: 7-day withdrawal delay (challenge window)

rows.label: Compute overhead
rows.value: Low — no proof generation overhead at all

rows.label: EVM compatibility
rows.value: ✅ Full EVM equivalence — deploy existing Solidity

rows.label: Trust model
rows.value: At least 1 honest watcher must monitor the chain

rows.label: Best for
rows.value: DeFi, general dApps needing EVM compatibility

l2Why.heading: Layer 2 — what it is and why we need it
l2Why.subtitle: Ethereum L1 is secure but small. A Layer 2 keeps that security while doing the heavy lifting somewhere cheaper.
l2Why.problemB: . When demand spikes, a simple swap can cost
l2Why.problemC: . Consumer apps, games and micro-payments simply can't live there.
l2Why.ideaC: back to L1. Ethereum stays the judge; the work happens where it's cheap.

steps.title: Execute off-chain
steps.text: Thousands of users transact on the L2 — fast and near-free. The L2 sequencer orders and runs them.

steps.title: Batch & prove
steps.text: The L2 compresses the batch and produces a proof of what happened (a fraud proof window, or a validity proof).

steps.title: Settle on L1
steps.text: The batch + proof is posted to Ethereum. L1 enforces correctness, so the L2 inherits L1-grade security.

layer2.heading: Layer 2 Scaling: Optimistic vs ZK Rollups
layer2.subtitleA: Both rollup types execute transactions off-chain and post results to Ethereum L1 — but they differ fundamentally in

op.title: Optimistic Rollups
op.introA: Transactions are posted to L1 with an
op.introB: that they are valid. A 7-day challenge window allows anyone to submit a
op.introC: if they detect an invalid state transition.

shared: Execute transactions off-chain, post compressed data to Ethereum L1
Inherit Ethereum's security — L1 is the final arbiter
10–100× cheaper than L1 for end users
Native bridges back to L1 (with different withdrawal times)
ERC-20 tokens and ETH work on both

choose.rest: Use Arbitrum or Optimism — fastest path to deploy existing Solidity.
Use a ZK rollup — users don't want to wait 7 days to withdraw.
ZK proofs are foundational — only ZK rollups enable transaction privacy by design.

l2apps.chooseBody: DeFi-mature build → Arbitrum. Public goods / rollup-as-a-service → Optimism. Consumer & social with Coinbase reach → Base. Games or proof-heavy compute → Starknet. The mechanism matters less than the ecosystem fit.

polkadot.heading: Polkadot — shared security for sovereign parachains
polkadot.subtitle: A Relay Chain that pools ~300 validators to secure all connected parachains simultaneously. Cross-chain calls between parachains use XCM — no bridge contract, no wrapped token.

architecture.title: Relay Chain
architecture.desc: The L0 backbone. ~300 validators (BABE block production + GRANDPA finality) secure every parachain slot simultaneously. Validators rotate across parachains, so no single chain can be attacked in isolation.

architecture.title: Parachains & Coretime
architecture.desc: Parachains are L1s that rent block-production time (Coretime) on the Relay Chain — replacing the old slot-auction model in 2024. Each parachain gets shared security without bootstrapping its own validator set.

architecture.title: XCM — Cross-Consensus Messaging
architecture.desc: A native message format for calls between parachains. Assets and instructions travel trustlessly — verified by the Relay Chain validators, not a bridge multisig. No lock-and-mint, no honeypot.

architecture.title: Bridges to external chains
architecture.desc: Snowbridge (Ethereum ↔ Polkadot) and other dedicated bridge parachains connect to chains outside the Relay Chain. These use traditional bridge security, distinct from XCM.

economics: Staked DOT secures the Relay Chain — validators are slashed for misbehaviour
Coretime is purchased with DOT (on-demand or bulk) — replaces slot auctions
Governance: OpenGov lets any DOT holder propose and vote on referenda

developments.rest: — slot auctions replaced by flexible block-time marketplace
— chains can buy seconds of block-time rather than full parachain slots
— next-gen Relay Chain redesign (proposed by Gavin Wood) for more general-purpose compute
— trustless Ethereum ↔ Polkadot bridge using on-chain light clients

polkadotEco.subtitle: Each parachain is sovereign — they specialise. EVM compatibility, DeFi hubs, enterprise smart contracts, trustless Bitcoin wrapping: the DOT ecosystem covers the full stack.

avalanche.heading: Avalanche — sub-second L0 with sovereign L1s
avalanche.subtitle: A three-chain Primary Network (X, P, C) with Snowman consensus achieving sub-second finality. Avalanche L1s (ex-Subnets) let teams deploy sovereign chains with their own validator subsets while retaining native Warp messaging.

architecture.title: Primary Network
architecture.desc: Three built-in chains: X-Chain (UTXO asset transfers), P-Chain (validator coordination, L1 creation), C-Chain (EVM — where most DeFi lives). All validators must validate the Primary Network.

architecture.title: Avalanche L1s (ex-Subnets)
architecture.desc: Custom chains with their own validator subsets. Renamed via ACP-13/77 in 2024 — chains no longer need to validate the Primary Network, reducing the AVAX stake requirement dramatically. Powers gaming chains, institutional deployments.

architecture.title: Snowman Consensus
architecture.desc: Probabilistic consensus where each validator samples a small random subset of peers. Converges in < 1 second with high confidence. Unlike BFT it scales well with validator count — 1,000+ validators without performance loss.

architecture.title: Avalanche Warp Messaging
architecture.desc: Native L1↔L1 messaging. The source L1's validator set signs the message; the destination verifies against the P-Chain's record of that validator set. No third-party bridge relayer needed.

economics: All C-Chain gas fees are burned in AVAX — deflationary pressure with network usage
Validators must stake ≥ 2,000 AVAX on the Primary Network
Avalanche L1s pay validators in any token — AVAX no longer required (ACP-77)

developments.rest: — chains no longer need to validate Primary Network; opens sovereign deployment at lower cost
— major fee reduction upgrade, making L1 deployment 99%+ cheaper
— dynamic fees and EIP-1559-style base fee on C-Chain
— production-ready cross-L1 messaging SDK for teams building multi-chain apps

avalancheEco.subtitle: The C-Chain hosts DeFi; sovereign L1s host games, institutional rails, and enterprise applications — each with its own validator set and economics, connected back via Warp.

privacy.heading: Privacy on a public ledger — why it's hard
privacy.subtitle: Public chains are transparent by default: every address, balance and transfer is visible to anyone, forever.
privacy.normalCaption: Anyone can read who paid whom, how much, and trace it backwards forever.
privacy.difficultyA: You can't just "encrypt the chain" — every validator must still
privacy.trickA: The trick: let validators confirm a transaction is
privacy.trickCaption: Every privacy project is a different recipe of cryptography to pull this off — next slide.

privacyApproaches.heading: Four ways to get privacy on-chain
privacyApproaches.subtitle: Each makes a different trade-off between strength, flexibility, and how regulators treat it.

items.name: Zcash
items.mech: Optional shielded transactions use zk-SNARKs to prove ownership and value conservation without revealing sender, recipient or amount.
items.trade: Privacy is opt-in, so most volume stays transparent — weakening the anonymity set.

items.name: Monero
items.mech: Mandatory privacy: ring signatures hide the sender, stealth addresses hide the recipient, RingCT hides the amount.
items.trade: No transparency option — auditing is hard, so many exchanges have delisted it.

items.name: Aztec · Aleo
items.mech: Smart contracts with private state and inputs — build any confidential dApp, not just private payments.
items.trade: Far more flexible, but newer tooling (Noir, Leo) and privacy lives at the contract level.

items.name: Mixers
items.mech: Pool many deposits, withdraw to fresh addresses — breaking the on-chain link between sender and receiver.
items.trade: Credibly neutral and immutable — which is exactly why they became a sanctions target.

privacyRegulation.heading: Privacy is the most contested area in crypto
privacyRegulation.subtitle: Non-custodial privacy code is being tested in court right now. Build with this reality in mind.

events.d: OFAC sanctioned an immutable smart contract — not a company. Legitimate users were caught alongside launderers.
Co-founders of a non-custodial privacy wallet were arrested. Writing privacy software became a legal flashpoint.
Courts are deciding where "privacy tool" ends and "money-laundering infrastructure" begins. No settled answer yet.

privacyRegulation.guidanceC: (e.g. prove "not on a sanctions list" without revealing identity) over all-or-nothing anonymity.

privacyFuture.heading: The upside — why ZK privacy is the next frontier
privacyFuture.subtitleA: ZK flips privacy from "hiding things" to
privacyFuture.subtitleB: — the unlock institutions and regulators have been waiting for.

items.title: Confidential DeFi & payments
items.d: Trade without broadcasting your size, run payroll or treasury on-chain without exposing salaries. Dark-pool-style execution with public settlement.

items.title: Reusable private identity
items.d: KYC once, then prove "is human", "over 18", or "not sanctioned" to any dApp — no document, no doxxing, no honeypot of personal data.

items.title: Proof without exposure
items.d: Exchanges prove solvency, funds prove eligibility, firms prove an audit passed — all without opening their books or counterparties.

items.title: Verifiable computation
items.d: ZK rollups, coprocessors and verifiable AI: prove an off-chain result was computed correctly, so chains trust heavy work they never ran.

privacyFuture.bannerA: Where this is going: privacy stops being a
privacyFuture.bannerC: — the path for regulated finance, healthcare and government to actually use public chains.

evaluate.heading: Evaluating a project — the team & the money
evaluate.subtitle: Three of six axes. Glance at the green vs red column — most failures are visible here first.

axes.title: Team & track record
green: Active GitHub, not just a whitepaper

axes.title: Contracts & audits
red: No audit, or stale vs current code

axes.title: Token distribution
red: Unlock cliffs that dump on retail

evaluate.next: Three more axes next — liquidity, marketing, and on-chain reality →

evaluate2.heading: Evaluating a project — liquidity & reality check
evaluate2.subtitle: The last three axes. Most can be checked in ten minutes with free tools.

axes.title: Liquidity & lock-ups
red: One thin pool — pull & it collapses

evaluate2.toolsBody: <0>Etherscan</0> (contracts, holders) · <1>DeFiLlama</1> (TVL, volume) · <2>Token Terminal</2> (revenue) · <3>Nansen / Arkham</3> (wallet flows) · <4>Dune</4> (custom analytics).

decisionExamples.heading: Platform selection — worked examples
decisionExamples.subtitle: Seven use cases walked through the five-question framework to a concrete recommendation. Read the "why" column to understand which axis drove the decision.

items.rec: Bitcoin Lightning · USDC on Base / Solana
items.why: Low cost · sub-second finality · users already trust USD-denominated value
L1 for vault security, L2 for user flow · deepest liquidity · most auditors available
Permissioned access · selective data sharing per channel · no token economics required
Programmable ZK privacy · verifiable claims without disclosure · Ethereum L1 settlement
High throughput · low gas per action · gaming-focused tooling · existing player ecosystems
Full control over VM, gas token, governance · trade-off: bootstrap your own validator set
Regulator-mandated participation · interop required at policy level · no public token needed

quiz.question: A user withdraws funds from Arbitrum (an optimistic rollup) back to Ethereum L1. The standard withdrawal takes ~7 days. Why?
options: L1 block time is slower than L2 block time, so L1 must catch up before processing the withdrawal.

Optimistic rollups assume transactions valid by default. The 7-day "challenge window" lets anyone submit a fraud proof if the L2 state is invalid; until that window closes, withdrawals can't be safely finalised on L1.
Arbitrum batches withdrawals weekly to amortise gas costs across users.
Ethereum L1 deliberately rate-limits L2 withdrawals to prevent rollup-induced congestion.
quiz.explanation: The 7-day delay is structural to the optimistic rollup model. The L2 sequencer posts state roots to L1 with the assumption they're valid; anyone watching the chain can challenge an invalid root by submitting a fraud proof during the challenge window. Until that window expires, L1 cannot trust the state root enough to release withdrawals. ZK rollups don't have this delay — each batch ships with a validity proof that L1 verifies cryptographically, so withdrawals can finalise in one L1 block. Third-party fast bridges (Across, Hop, Stargate) let users skip the wait by paying liquidity providers a small premium up front.

quiz2.question: A team is launching a new app-specific blockchain. They need full control over consensus algorithm, gas token, and upgrade governance. Which model best fits — and what's the trade-off?
options: Polkadot parachain via Coretime — sovereignty within the relay chain's shared security envelope, no trade-off.
Cosmos zone via Cosmos SDK — full sovereignty over consensus, gas, and upgrades; the trade-off is bootstrapping their own validator set without inheriting any external security.
Avalanche L1 — sovereign chains, but they must use Snowman consensus and pay AVAX-denominated subscription fees.
All three give equal sovereignty; the decision is purely cost-driven.
quiz2.explanation: Cosmos zones are sovereign by design. Each chain picks its own consensus engine (CometBFT is default, but anything goes), its own gas token, its own governance and upgrade rules. The trade-off is real: the team must recruit validators, design economic security, and bootstrap a token of value to pay them — no shared security comes for free. Polkadot parachains share the relay chain's validator set (great), but must conform to the relay's runtime model and Coretime schedule (less sovereignty than Cosmos). Avalanche L1s (formerly Subnets, renamed via ACP-13/77 in 2024) do allow custom VMs and validator subsets, but in practice most run Snowman variants and pay validator fees in AVAX. The honest framing: shared security versus sovereignty is a trade-off, not a tier list.

takeaways.title: Section 05 — Key Takeaways
items: ZK rollups use cryptographic validity proofs — no challenge window, instant finality, and the foundation for verifiable privacy
Optimistic rollups (Arbitrum, Base, Optimism) offer full EVM compatibility with lower computation cost — at the price of a 7-day withdrawal delay
Each L2 has a distinct culture: Arbitrum for DeFi depth, Base for consumer apps, Starknet for games and ZK-native compute
Privacy on public chains requires extra cryptographic machinery — the regulatory line for "privacy tooling" is still being drawn
Run five questions before picking a platform: permission, programmability, throughput, privacy, and sovereignty

teamCheckpoint.title: Find your problem statement
teamCheckpoint.subtitle: You've now seen the platform landscape. Time to converge on the specific problem your team wants to tackle — before Day 3 turns it into a concrete smart-contract design.

have.item1B: from Day 1 (find them now if you skipped it).
have.item2: The pain points each member brought into Day 2.
have.item3: An understanding of platform trade-offs — public vs permissioned, L1 vs L2, throughput vs cost.

do.item1: Each member pitches their top pain point (60 seconds each, no debate yet).
do.item2B: — specific enough to design for, broad enough that everyone is engaged.
do.item3Italic: "Today, <who> struggles with <what> because <why>. We want to design a smart-contract solution that …"
do.item4: Capture three current pain metrics if you can — cost, time, error rate, trust gap, etc.

bring.item2: A first guess at the industry (you'll refine it Day 3 morning).
bring.item3: An open mind — the Day 3 decision framework may tell you blockchain isn't the right tool.

teamCheckpoint.footnoteRest: Business Applications for Smart Contracts will run your problem through a structured decision framework, walk you through five industry verticals, then have you sketch a full design as a team. The project deliverable starts there.
